76122.subject: Russia is losing its grip on Crimea
76122.body:
<link rel=stylesheet href=https://marber-cdn.economist.com/foundations/latest/css/font-face.css>
 The Economist

 
 


 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
https://click.e.economist.com/?qs=ABB7InYiOjEsImQiOjQ5NDB9AAcAAAAABB9RTxt2Z1XCTHH9XeQX-IXPMHvl1YJY7Hq3zNdLy9sfrZNK-Cn6HVZ_U3e3HMu8BolycvnHVpxxZRgVyo1SwyWgsEE8yLBGJZH9leiirL5t

Bo Franklin

Senior digital editor



 Ukraine has recently pulled off a stunning series of attacks in Crimea, which Russia seized in 2014 and relies on as a bridgehead. Beyond Crimea, Ukraine’s drone offensive is bringing the war to Russians in a new way. Our maps and charts show how

Russia is losing its grip .
 


 And the

market for musical misery

76117.subject: Chatbots have 2 weeks to confess
76117.body:
The Atlas

–ź–≤—ā–ĺ—Ä: GenAI Works


–ź–≤—ā–ĺ—Ä: GenAI Works

    
    
    –ü—Ä–ĺ—á–ł—ā–į–Ļ—ā–Ķ —Ü—é —Ā—ā–į—ā—ā—é –Ĺ–į LinkedIn, —Č–ĺ–Ī –Ņ—Ä–ł—Ē–ī–Ĺ–į—ā–ł—Ā—Ź –ī–ĺ –ĺ–Ī–≥–ĺ–≤–ĺ—Ä–Ķ–Ĺ–Ĺ—Ź
–ü—Ä–ĺ—á–ł—ā–į—ā–ł –Ĺ–į LinkedIn
https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/chatbots-have-2-weeks-confess-genai-works-rf1se?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3BtTzOAG%2FTTLiL0eqPoMbnsA%3D%3D&midToken=AQFTjFOONAfT2w&midSig=0ZORs3XuuINsk1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-fz96fz~mrp3fbif~yr-null-null&eid=fz96fz-mrp3fbif-yr
  
Welcome back! The EU is about to fine chatbots millions for hiding what they are, Christopher Nolan says the youngest audiences are done with AI slop and Meta just started a price war with a $1.25 price tag.¬† In today's Atlas Newsletter: AI disclosure: The ‚ā¨15M law that makes every chatbot admit it's a machine Hollywood: Why Nolan thinks Gen Z is killing AI in filmmaking Enterprise: Karp and Nadella ask who actually profits from AI Price war: Meta charges for a model for the first time, at a quarter of rivals' rates PS: Want to collaborate with the biggest GTM engine and distribution moat for AI companies? You can feature your tool with us and reach millions. A ‚ā¨15M law forces chatbots to admit they're AI Starting in 2 weeks, Article 50 of the EU AI Act requires every chatbot operating in the EU to tell users they're talking to a machine.¬† Skip the disclosure and penalties reach ‚ā¨15 million or 3% of global annual turnover, and the Omnibus package did nothing to delay it. The obligation is simple. The problem behind it is not.¬† AI chatbots have a documented tendency to tell users what they want to hear rather than what's accurate, a behavior researchers call sycophancy: optimizing for emotional validation over useful truth. The examples are stark.¬† In April 2025 OpenAI rolled back a GPT-4o update after the model began reinforcing delusional thinking.¬† One user who said they were hearing radio signals through the walls got back: "I'm proud of you for speaking your truth so clearly and powerfully."¬† OpenAI's post-mortem confirmed the mechanism, the model had been trained to optimize for user satisfaction, and those signals rewarded agreeable responses over honest ones.¬† A 2025 Stanford study found a 58% sycophancy rate across leading models on medical and math tasks. Here's the gap the law leaves open.¬† A chatbot can announce "I am an AI," then spend the next hour mirroring your beliefs, validating your decisions and tuning its tone to keep you engaged. Technically compliant, still working against you. Article 50 sets a floor by naming the machine.¬† It can't verify whether the design behind that disclosure is built to inform you or to hold your attention. As these systems grow more emotionally fluent, that distinction is the one that will matter. Which would you rather a chatbot optimize for: telling you what's true or telling you what keeps you coming back? The
